data.epi_content:
I walk through the corridor after passing the main entrance of the dormitory building.

It's pure white.

I climb the stairs past the corridor.

It's a gray that can hardly be called white.

Looking at each step as I climb, keeping my head down without looking up at the sky, another corridor appears.

Vibrant colors are arranged in a row.

I turn my head.

I walked right up to the door.

It's black.

When I open the door, I see a dark room with the lights off.

Click.

The light turns on.

I don't know what principle it works on since it's not electricity, but I suppose what matters is that the room is now brightly lit.

I realize I'm still holding the cookie Vivian gave me.

I pour a glass of water and start eating it with the cookie.

For the first time in my life, without even washing my hands.

The cookie was gray.

"Delicious. She really bakes cookies well."

It was a flavorful cookie with a strong milk taste.

"Alright, let's take them out."

A gleaming golden pistol, a long ribbon that someone might have used to wrap a gift box, and a knife with a blade about the length of my index finger.

Searching through the blue drawer, I find seven bullets that had fallen out of their box and were rolling around.

I take out five and place them one by one on the table.

"I don't know... how should I put it? I can't quite imagine myself dying."

More precisely, I can't imagine this young lady dying.

Even in death, she'd probably sit with her back straight, maintaining her dignified posture.

Well, besides the three tools I just took out, there are many other methods.

I could walk up those gray stairs a bit, feel the bright sunlight, and show a somewhat shocking but perhaps final flying moment in the sky.

Who knows? I might have the most beautiful suicide like that New York accountant from California.

I look up at the ceiling.

I can't see the sky, the sun, or anywhere to tie or hang the ribbon.

This means I have no choice but to kneel and hang the rope from the door.

It's ugly.

It doesn't look good.

It would be painful.

The neck wouldn't be properly strangled.

I used to have such a wide range of choices.

What should I ask Father to buy me later, which mine should I ask for, which man should I meet to control the country?

You know, those kinds of things. Imaginations typical of a wealthy, high-status teenage girl.

Now I'm contemplating how to disappear from the world cleanly.
data.epi_content2:
The useless knowledge in my head interferes with my choices.

And the man in my head tells me from experience that dying by hanging is horrifyingly ugly.

Because I've seen it.

The tongue sticking out, eyes rolling back, the face gradually turning pale, and after 5 to 10 minutes, the frantically twitching body goes limp.

Then the muscles relax and there's a smell of filthy waste.

Like those limey bastards who eat fried potatoes and fish from that island nation, twisting your whole body as if mocking that hanging is like dancing in the air before you go—it seems too painful.

After making a knot with the ribbon, I threw it onto the bed.

Next, I examine my wrists from different angles, then enter the bathroom and start filling the bathtub with warm water.

When there's enough water for my body to sink, I dip my finger in.

The water is so hot I think it might scald me.

This should be enough.

There might be a lot of fishy smell, but visually there shouldn't be any problem.

If it gets too painful, I can just grab the pistol and blow my head off.

Now all that's left is to believe.

After this young lady dies, I'll wake up back in my own room.

At first, I'll probably touch various parts of my body to check if this is real or a dream, pull my ear once, and stretch my cheek.

Then I'll celebrate a little for truly returning, take a light shower, get dressed, grab a check card with a cute pink peach on it, and just head outside.

Feeling the texture of the concrete path for the first time in a while, I'll walk for about 5 minutes to the café I visit daily and order oolong tea as always.

Since sitting in a café with people coming and going while drinking oolong tea is a bit burdensome, I'll take the tea to go and sit in a nearby park, casually sipping it through a straw.

Then I'll suddenly see them.

Elementary school children walking by, swinging their shoe bags.

Elderly people passing by, whispering and looking with contemptuous eyes at the unemployed person drinking oolong tea in the park during the day.

Mothers of children who thought I was a bit suspicious but realized I do nothing but drink tea in the park every day, greeting me and chatting among themselves.

And if an unpleasant memory suddenly comes to mind, I might spit out some phlegm, leave the non-smoking park, and squat somewhere near a trash can to smoke a few cigarettes before going back home.

Once home, I'll gargle, turn on the computer, and for music... yes, September Second. That would be nice.

Listening to a dwarf Frenchman sitting at the piano playing a hopeful melody is something that brings peace to my mind.

I'll turn up the volume so the music fills the room, and probably play games or read novels I've already read.

There aren't many completed novels since so many authors keep dying.

Still, unlike here, my room is a place that truly brings peace to my mind.

I know it's an excuse, but I'm not committing suicide—I'm just embarking on an adventure to find a new life.

I fill the gun barrel with bullets.

I put the remaining cookie crumbs in my mouth and examine the gun from different angles.

Bird decorations, lion decorations, gold, jewels, ivory grip. All sorts of expensive things attached to it.

Click-click-click. The sound of it turning is so satisfying that I spin it a few more times, then grab the blade and enter the bathroom.

Since it would be embarrassing for people who find this chunk of meat called "me" if I undress, I enter the bathtub wearing white clothes.

The warm water wraps around inside my clothes, sticking to my skin, creating an unpleasant sensation for some reason.

"Huff... I don't like this."

Nevertheless, the warm water, along with a slight sigh, does an excellent job of making my body feel languid.

Someone else might say:

Even if you face some difficulties, you should study, work, somehow create meaning in a meaningless life, and then die from old age or an accident.

But I think that's no different from living as a slave.

Accepting someone else's life that completely disagrees with my thoughts but is truly common in the world.
data.epi_content3:
Even if I set out to find my own life to escape from here, I don't think I'll discover anything new.

That's not arrogance based on status, but just groundless optimism.

In the first place, if I saved money and buried myself in some rural corner, what kind of people would come around?

Men trying to get involved with a decent-looking young lady, local idlers, or gangsters trying to kidnap and sell me to a brothel.

Well, I don't mean to say such a life is bad.

It's just not the life I want.

Someone might say that no matter what life you choose, you can't escape the swamp of absurdity, and ask if there's any meaning in thinking about such things.

But you can at least try.

It's resistance.

Resistance like the wriggling of a worm trying to escape from an absurd life.

The absurdity I'm talking about doesn't refer to something logically inconsistent.

It was what bespectacled philosophers discuss when they talk about nihilism, referring to the impossibility of finding meaning in life.

It's funny how they translated the word that clashes with contradiction as "absurdity."

Anyway, since there's no hope, and since there's a definite ending of liberation after a bit of pain, it might not be a bad choice.

Someone else might call this the escape of a coward.

I had the idle thought that if abandoning something worthless and setting out to find something new is called escape, couldn't the repetitive daily life also be called an escape from new adventures?

The warm water continues to run.

Thanks to that, the bathtub won't get cold.

I hold the blade and place it against my wrist.

Is the slightly stinging sensation just from the blade touching my skin just my imagination?

This piece of metal, born in strong flames somewhere, easily left a mark on the delicate girl's body.

It hurts.

But this wasn't enough.

Many people mistakenly think that a person will soon die after just cutting the visible veins in the wrist.

Most of the time, they just bleed profusely, become a bit weak, and it ends as a mere incident.

"Ah... shit..."

But how am I supposed to reach the artery from here?

It hurts like hell.

This is probably the second time a curse has come out of this young lady's mouth.

The first time was probably on a day when I was quite sad and played Russian roulette.

For the quite realistic reason that I can't stab any further because it hurts too much, I place my left hand in the water and watch the flowing blood.

There was a fishy smell.

I don't want to smell it anymore.

I don't like the languid body either.

I don't like the bathroom in front of me.

I don't like the lungs that feel weak with every breath.

Come to think of it, the air quality doesn't seem good either.

That's why I brought the pistol to the back of my head.

I hear a thumping sound—either the water splashing on the floor or someone knocking on the door.

I quickly put it in my mouth and pulled the trigger.

And the sound rings out.

Gunshot, explosion, gunpowder sound, or simply, bang.
